text: Mason villagers are garbage cans. And you're probably thinking, "What did that poor mason do to you?" But I actually mean this as a good thing. Mason villagers are super useful like a garbage can because they take a bunch of your trash items. First, when mining, you often wind up with tons of cobblestone that you don't need. But if you smelt that, you can trade 20 stone for an emerald with a mason. And if you get a silk touch pickaxe, you can get emeralds even easier. But they get better because when you level them up, they have a chance to buy granite, andite, and diorite from you, which is amazing because these blocks rarely get used, but are everywhere. So, at least you can turn them into emeralds. And best of all, one really common way to get XP is by mining quartz. But if you do this, you often get way more quartz than you need. But you can trade quartz with a mason for emeralds and get extra XP for

Mojang just solved the debate over putting sharpness or smite on your sword because of their new updates. Let me explain. People argue about whether your main sword should be sharpness or smite in single player because sharpness does a little extra damage to everything, letting you kill everything slightly faster. But smite does a lot of extra damage to undead mobs, letting you one-shot most of them. And both enchantments have their merits because undead mobs make up a huge portion of the mobs you'll encounter. So being able to mow them down quickly is great, but living mobs are often harder to kill. But Mojang has made a few changes recently that crown smite the winner. First, they've been adding tons of new undead mobs to encounter like the bogged parched camel husk zombie nautilus and zombie horse. And these undead mobs are trickier to kill than most since some of them have spears letting them move really fast and do tons of damage. And on top of that, Mojang just made it so 5% of zombies spawn with up to 100 health. So unless you want to hit a zombie seven times with sharpness, use smite.

you don't know about this insanely rare villager. You're probably thinking of the jungle villager or a villager riding a chicken, but everyone knows about those. And until a few days ago, I had never even heard of this villager. And I bet you haven't either. That's because this villager is only in the 2025 April Fool's snapshot craft mine, where you have to unlock different features of the game by playing through mines. But villagers are insanely rare to begin with because they can't be unlocked like most other mobs. The only way to find them is by finding a village. But villages don't generate in any of the normal mines, and zombie villagers don't spawn either, making it nearly impossible to get villagers. And on top of that, beating this update doesn't require you to find villagers. So, the only person I saw that found this villager is someone who wanted to beat 100% of this update. They found it by doing one of the special boss fights, but ignoring the boss and flying away to a village because boss fight maps are the only ones that spawn structures. And once they found a villager, they put down a load stone, which turns the villager into a vindicator that sells illagers called the traitor.

The easiest way to move villagers in Minecraft. I just saw this video from Exumavoid that shows how to move villagers without boats or mine carts. Basically, you break all the job site blocks in a village and then craft a bunch of composters and place them in a line in the direction you want the villagers to go. Then you break the composters from the start of the line to the end. And as you do this, you'll break the villagers job site blocks so they'll move down the line to claim another job. And this is pretty good, but it only works during the day and on non-nitwit adult villagers. So, if you want to kidnap babies or lazies, you can. And it requires a ton of composters. So, here's a cheaper option that works in both Java and Bedrock. First, go around the village and break all the beds and break the bells. Then, place a bell in a bed where you want the villagers to go and ring the bell. Ringing the bell causes villagers to go to their beds. And since you destroyed all the beds, they'll go to the bed you placed. Then run forward, place another bed, run back and ring the bell. And then destroy the bed and all the villagers will walk to the next bed you placed.

You're wrong about the villager trading rework. Hear me out. Over 2 years ago, Mojang released an extremely controversial experimental change to villager trading. The main change was that librarians would only sell a few enchantments depending on what biome they were from. So to get enchanted books, you'd need to find a few different villages. And people hated this because they thought it would be grindier than just re-rolling trades. But while far from perfect, this update got so much undeserved hate. First, people hated that finding villages would take just as much luck as re-rolling trades. But it wouldn't because Mojang made cgraphers trade maps to other village types so you could find villages much easier. Next, people hated that they'd need to transport villagers around for trading, but you could just trade with them there. And we have happy guests now, so this wouldn't be that big of a deal. And finally, people hated that this update made enchanted books harder to get. And they're right. Things like needing to trade with a villager until it's max level just to get protection three is terrible. But this change is so much more fun than rolling villagers. And I would know because I spent 30 minutes to not even get the right enchantment in the video above the title.

This bug makes vindicators even deadlier than normal on Java Edition. Let me explain. Vindicators are already super dangerous because they do the fourth most damage in the game. Meaning in hard difficulty with iron armor, they twoshot you. But there's a bug that makes them even stronger called double hitting. After a mob hits you, there's supposed to be a cool down before it hits you again. But sometimes mobs will skip this cool down and hit you a second time really fast. This is especially dangerous with vindicators because they can hit you twice and kill you almost instantly. But it's even worse for shields. Shields are supposed to protect you from their hits, and they do sometimes, but a lot of the time, the Vindicator will double hit, disabling your shield with the first hit and then doing damage to you with the second, even though you were blocking.

Mojang keeps fixing bugs that no one asked them to fix, and it's terrible. First, a few months ago, Mojang released a snapshot, and in it, they fixed a bug that has been in the game for so long that no one even knew it was a bug, and everyone just assumed it was a feature. Normally, when you sneak, if you open a gooey, it unsneaks you. But if you turned on toggle sneak, you were able to stay sneaked while in inventories. This was super useful for building traps and secret bases since unsneaking would show everyone your name tag and give away your location. But apparently, this was a bug. And so even though removing this bug wouldn't improve the game in any way. And over 4,000 people petitioned for Mojang to not make this change, they made it anyway. And there's no easy workaround. Like forcing yourself to shift by being under a slab still shows your name. So you need invisibility potions all the time. But it gets worse because I just found out that Mojang made a change that makes flying with an elytra way worse. You know how fun it is to fly through the Nether dodging trees? Well, it's ruined now because you used to fly right through vines, but now hitting any climbable block instantly stops you from flying and can make you fall to your

The best and worst bugs were just fixed and barely anyone's talking about it. Let me explain. Two snapshots ago, Mojang added a ton of cool stuff like the Camelusk and the parched, but they also fixed some important bugs. First, there's been a bug on Java Edition for almost 15 years that made items have tiny gaps between their pixels. So, players used a mod to fix the gaps, but Mojang just fixed this bug, so now we don't have to. But Mojang also fixed a bug that allowed for a feature that everyone loved. On Bedrock Edition, if you throw a trident into a pit and move it with pistons, the trident will continuously hit mobs. But on Java, the trident can only hit one mob before it stops hitting, which makes no sense. Like, why does this trident not hit the villager? But a month ago, when the first mounts of Mayhem snapshot dropped, it added a bug that made it so trident, arrows, and other projectiles will get caught on entities and deal damage insanely fast, continuously. But this bug also let Trident hit more than one mob, making Trident killers work on Java. But Mojang just fixed this bug, meaning Trident killers will remain a Bedrock exclusive.

A new enchantment called Lunge just got added to Minecraft and it's really strong. Here's everything you need to know about it. The Lunge enchantment can be applied to the new spear weapon and when the player attacks, it launches the player forward. It doesn't work while swimming in water or while flying, but you can lunge forward and then immediately start flying to gain a lot of speed. Interestingly enough, it does work while crawling. The player doesn't need to be touching the ground to lunge either, and the player can use it multiple times while falling. The lunge enchantment goes to level three, launching the player further with each level. Lower tiers of spears have shorter attack cooldowns, meaning you can lunge more often than using a higher tier of spear. But there's a catch. Every time you lunge, it does durability to the spear. And the higher level of lunge, the more durability it uses, dealing up to 128 durability per lunge. This means a wooden spear can only lunge once and a netherite spear 16 times or around 60 with unbreaking three. And the lunge enchantment is incompatible with mending, meaning you only have so many lunges before you need to repair your spear. So, diamond spears will probably be the meta for the lunch since it's much easier to repair them than netherite.

The camel husk, parched, lunge buffs, and more just got added in the newest snapshot. Here's what changed. First, the camel husk got added, which acts like a zombie horse and has a husk with a spear riding it. But to make it even harder, the camel husk also has a new mob, the parched, riding it, which is a desert variant of a skeleton that slowly shoots weakness arrows from the back of the camel husk. The parched also spawns randomly around the desert biome. Just like the husk, these mobs will only spawn in the desert and they won't burn during the day. So, the desert will be a much more dangerous biome than it used to be. If you kill the riders of the camel husk, you can tame the camel husk for yourself. Also, horses, camels, donkeys, and mules just got a big buff, allowing them to swim when the player is riding them instead of sinking and kicking the player off of them. And finally, the lunge enchantment, which launches the player forward, just got a huge buff and a tiny nerf. The lunge enchantment now takes one durability per use, but it's also way stronger because it used to take three, four, or five hunger to use the different lunge levels, and now it's 1, two, or Three.

The Minecraft competitor Hightail just released and here are my thoughts. First, I'm really impressed by how much fun this game was. The combat is way different from Minecraft and reminds me of hero shooters like Marvel Rivals and Overwatch because you have a few different things you can do with your weapons. And if you use your weapon enough, you get a special ability that does a ton of damage. Hightail seems much more catered towards people who like exploring and fighting than building because the progression is much less simple, but not in a bad way. To upgrade your armor, it can take hours because some ores only spawn in certain biomes, and you need certain mob drops to upgrade your gear, requiring you to take on enemies before you're really strong. There are a ton of structures to explore, and while they don't give that useful of stuff, it's still a lot of fun. My main complaint, though, is that some parts of this game are just annoying. Like, crafting takes time, so to turn a bunch of hide into leather to craft armor, you have to wait several minutes, and this just isn't fun. The game is generally pretty good though and it's like a cross between Minecraft, Terraria, Arc and Overwatch.

Night vision is now the worst potion in Minecraft because Mojang just ruined it. Night vision is already pretty unused because it requires the player to go to the Nether and get a bunch of resources to get it. And by the time you're at the brewing stage, you've probably already done most of the mining in your world. So, you don't really need night vision. But worse than that, after all your work, you only get 24 minutes of night vision. They take up a bunch of slots in your inventory since they don't stack. And so many players use fullbrite or just put down torches. But even worse than that, night vision makes no sense. For some reason, night vision makes darker areas brighter and brighter areas darker instead of just making everything uniformly bright. This is pretty much unnoticeable if you're normal and use the highest brightness setting. But if you play with lower brightness, it makes torches darker than a pitch black area, which makes no sense. Also, above ground has moonlight, so it should be pretty bright with night vision. But it isn't, and caves are brighter for some reason. But worst of all, people who used bright brightness never used to have to deal with this. But in the newest versions of Minecraft, Mojang made night vision way darker by making night vision look like moody brightness no matter

New baby mob textures, craftable name tags, and more were just added to Minecraft in the first snapshot of the new year. Here's everything that changed. First, name tags are no longer a difficult item to obtain since you can craft them using one paper and one copper, iron, or gold nugget placed diagonally like this. Next, adult rabbits got an overhaul and now look completely different, have new animations, and a much larger hitbox. I have a pet rabbit, so I'm qualified to say this. This rabbit is a chonker and needs to lay off the carrots. Also, their faces shouldn't be flat since rabbits have big noses, but overall, the new texture and animation is pretty good. Additionally, wolves, cats, and pigs got new baby sounds. And finally, wolves, cats, pigs, cows, sheep, chickens, ocelots, and rabbits got new baby variants. And it's going to be way harder to kill their parents in front of them now because look how cute they are. Puppies, kittens, piglets, calves, lambs, chicks, ocelot kittens, bunnies.

This might be the worst Minecraft player of all time. This YouTuber has spent almost 30 hours trying to beat Minecraft hardcore lately, and he still hasn't succeeded. Whether it's his slow 40-year-old reaction times or just a skill issue, he just can't seem to kill the dragon. So, here are some tips. First, he died pillaring up to the end crystals because he placed down water and then got knocked away from it, so he couldn't clutch. So, you should always bring a second water bucket. That way, you can make an infinite water source and put down water around the end for you to land in when the dragon knocks you up. Next, he died to the dragon charging him and hitting him three times, which instantly killed him. This could have been easily prevented using a shield or by shooting it with a bow, but Ledwig completely ignored it while it was perched. The dragon only charges the player when it's leaving its perch. So, when the dragon is on the end fountain, be ready for it to charge at you. And finally, Lwig is convinced that you can't melee the dragon because it knocks you away when you try. But he's running at its head, and if you run under its wings or tail, it can't hit you away. And then you can hit it from in the end fountain where it can't hit you back unless you

These are some of the coolest redstone contraptions I've ever seen, and the creator is so underrated. They're designed by Tasty, who keeps using insanely niche mechanics to build such cool stuff. First, you may have seen a ton of videos of people designing Redstone fans a few months ago. But this design is super simple and looks amazing because if you get a happy gas on a lead and stretch it really far, the happy gas starts spinning and looks like a fan blade. But even cooler than that, Tasty also designed water particle cannons that can look so good in any water themed build. They work because when a minecart touches water, it splashes water in the direction it's moving. And the more minecarts you stack, the bigger the splash gets, letting you spray people with water. But coolest of all, they just designed the most oreful doors in Minecraft. Normal redstone doors can look pretty cool, but these doors have smooth animations. They work by having a bunch of boats led to one boat that's far away. So when you open the door, the main boat moves and all the other boats follow. They aren't perfect, though, because you can force your way through and mobs can get stuck in them.

The creeper has been getting a ton of hate lately, and I'm here to defend it. Mojang recently said that based on their current design rules, they wouldn't add the creeper to modern Minecraft, and it's caused a lot of people to criticize the creeper. But I think the creeper is the perfect mob. Most mobs in Minecraft have the same strategy for fighting them. Just run at them swinging your sword, and it's really boring. But the creeper is unique because it blows up if you stay near it, which makes the player fight it by strategically retreating before hitting it again. And the creeper compliments other mobs so well because while a skeleton alone isn't very threatening, when a creeper is next to it, it becomes a much more unique challenge. Next, the creeper makes the player feel more than any other mob. When you see and kill a zombie, you probably feel nothing. But a creeper can scare almost any player and can make them mad by blowing up their stuff. And these feelings are a good thing because they make it so much more satisfying when you kill one. And finally, Minecraft is a game about building. So, an enemy that tries to stop you from building is the perfect enemy.

This is an immortality machine and it's been going viral lately because it makes it impossible to kill you if you stand in it since every time you pop a totem, you pick up another one instantly. And a few days ago, I saw this video from Carves where he points out that after 5 minutes, all the totems will despawn, making the machine useless. So, he shows how to build a better immortality machine and builds this. Not only does this look way less cool than the normal immortality machine, but it completely doesn't work. In the normal immortality machine, you pick up a new totem the instant your old totem gets popped. But in Carve's design, he cycles the totems through droppers to make sure they don't despawn, which is fine. But the speed that the totems are thrown to the player leaves a four game tick gap where you can be killed after each totem. So to actually become immortal, build this. First, dig a hole like this. Then break these three blocks. Put two droppers facing up and then a dropper facing into this gap. Then put a hopper here facing into the dropper. Put a hopper mine cart on top of it. Push a mud block into the hopper mine cart and put mud around it. Then build an observer clock pointing into the middle dropper. Place water in the corners and put slabs around the border. Now throw your totems in and they'll never despawn while making you immortal.

The spear's exclusive enchantment, Lunge, just got a huge change along with a few other changes. Here's what changed. First, the charge attack now does more damage when both the player and mobs use it. I tested this with a player and it did the same damage as it used to, but zombie horse jockeyies did way more damage from around three damage to around 10 damage depending on speed. So, zombie horses might actually be a big threat if you run into one early game, which I love. Next, the spear now gives visual feedback when it hits a mob, which is a nice touch. But most importantly, the lunge enchantment got a huge rework. The lunge enchantment launches the player forward when used, which allows for some crazy movement potential. But using the lunge used to take a ton of durability. With lunge three on a spear, it took 128 durability per lunge, meaning you only had about 16 lunges with a netherite spear, or around 60 if you had unbreaking on it. But the spear was repairable with mending. So every 60 lunges you'd have to repair it. But Mojang just changed this. So you'll no longer need to repair your spear often, but instead eat food often.

A massive new snapshot just dropped. Here's everything that changed. Get your notes ready because there's a lot. First, horses, donkeys, mules, zombie horses, and skeleton horses got new baby models and textures. And that's it.

Has this ever happened to you? Oh boy, I love building. Just got to finish lighting up my house. Oh no, I'm four torches short. Well, here's how to get those four torches easily. First, chop down some trees. Then, kill some animals so you have food. Then, make a furnace and smelt one of your wood into charcoal and use that charcoal to cook your food. Then, make some sticks using the leftover wood. Then, go into your mine and mine some coal and use it to smelt some iron, which you need for a bucket. Then, get water and find a lava pool so you can go to the Nether. Find a bastion and steal the piglin's gold to trade it with them for ender pearls. Then find a fortress and make your way to the blaze spawner, killing any mobs you need to along the way. Kill some blazes for blaze powder and leave the Nether. Then use your ender pearls and blaze powder to make eyes of ender and find the stronghold. Once inside, run around until you find the portal room. Once you find it, use the sticks you crafted earlier to make some arrows. Then go to the end and use your bow to take down all the end crystals. Then kill the ender dragon. And once it dies, you can finally craft some torches, go through the end portal, and finish your

The spear has a secret that makes it even stronger than everyone thinks. Everyone's been talking about the spear, and for good reason, because it's an insane weapon. It's so versatile because it lets you hit enemies from further away than a sword. It can hit multiple mobs at a time with its jab attack, which essentially adds sweeping edge to bedrock. And it can use the new lunge enchantment, which now takes hunger instead of durability and means that with a wooden spear, you can move really fast. But most importantly, it can charge, which does damage based on how fast the player is going. It's pretty similar to a mace, but so much easier to use because flying is way easier to control than falling. It does less damage than a mace, but it's still really strong, dealing over four hearts when flying. But before I tell you the secret, I just dropped merch. Look how cool it is. The secret is that while everyone thinks the spear deals damage based off speed, no one noticed that it's based on relative speed. This means that you don't have to be the one moving to do a ton of damage. If someone is flying at you with a spear, you can hit them back with your spear, and your spear will do more damage since they don't have a chest plate

This is the worst bone meal farm in Minecraft history. Let me explain. Sweet berry bushes are one of the most useless items in Minecraft, having no crafting recipes and being a terrible food source since they only give you one hunger bar. But they can be composted to get tons of bone meal and can be farmed insanely fast with a super simple design. It's literally just a grass block, a sweet berry bush, and 641 hoppers to compost all the berries. To use the farm, all you need is a really fast auto clicker because sweet berries can be bone meal multiple times per game tick. This means if you click 64 times per game tick, you can bone meal the sweet berries 4,600,000 times per hour. This is roughly 2,667 shulker boxes of bone meal per hour, or what? 2,300 bone meal farms would get you. And it gets you over 5,700,000 sweet berries per hour. And if you compost these sweet berries, you get 274,000 bone meal per hour.

Vindicators can be way more dangerous on Bedrock than Java. Let me explain. You might have seen me talk recently about how vindicators can completely ignore shields on Java Edition, making them super dangerous. And Bedrock vindicators can't do this, making them weaker than Java's Vindicators by default. But if the player helps, they can be way more dangerous than on Java. For example, if you name a Vindicator Johnny, it will attack pretty much every mob in the game except for a few. Vindicators won't attack other vindicators or pillagers, but they also won't attack baby villagers, but only on Java because on Bedrock, they have no problem with this. But vindicators can be even more dangerous because you can give them gear. If you get some diamond armor and enchant it with protection 4 and thorns, you can put the armor onto a vindicator using a dispenser. This armor actually works and is fully invisible, so you can surprise other players with insanely strong vindicators.

One of the most hated ideas in Minecraft history, the villager trading rebalance might be coming soon. Let me explain. The villager trading rework was an update that primarily changed librarian villagers, making them no longer have access to every enchanted book in the game, but instead have a few specific books depending on what biome they were from. This change was intended to make trading for enchanted books more fun by replacing the tedious re-rolling of villager trades with exploring to find a villager that will give you the trade you want without re-rolling much. And it was meant to make enchanted book trading more balanced since right now it's pretty easy to get infinite of any enchanted books you want. But Mojang went too far and made a lot of the enchanted books really hard to get, like requiring a swamp villager to get mending and requiring you to level librarians all the way up for certain enchantments. and they aren't even max level. People hated this. So, for 2 years, Mojang barely did anything involving this rebalance. But in the newest snapshot, Mojang fixed a ton of bugs related to the villager trading rebalance. So they might be working on it

The most dangerous biome in Minecraft is about to be the desert because of the camel husk. The camel husk is a new mob that spawns with a husk holding a spear and a parched riding it. Unlike the zombie horse jockey, camel husk spawn really frequently with 10% of husks spawning as a jockey, meaning you'll see these guys everywhere. And even more dangerous, the camel husk, parched, and husk don't burn in daylight. So, you'll be seeing them all the time. But it gets more dangerous because you basically have to fight it. The Camel Husk is almost as fast as the player and has the parched shooting at you to slow you down, so it's really hard to outrun, especially with mobs and terrain getting in your way, and you can't pillar up or dig down to escape it since the parched can shoot you. And even more dangerous than that, the spear that the husk is holding does damage based on speed, and the husk is traveling pretty fast. So, if you get hit, it can do about three hearts with iron armor on. But most dangerous of all, if you try to fight it with a sword and make the mistake of running towards it, this lets it do even more damage, one-shotting you in iron armor.

The most useless thing I've ever found in Minecraft is actually useful. Let me explain. A few weeks ago, I invented my Magnum Opus, a farm that gets zero items per hour, but gives the player the satisfaction of killing villagers no matter where they are in the world. And you might think that that's easy. Just chunkload an area with villager breeders and drop them from high up. But that doesn't give the player kill credit. My design does, meaning that without doing anything, my villagers killed statistic is going up. This works because I found that a piercing arrow can pierce five mobs before breaking. But if you send it through a Nether portal, its counter resets, letting you pierce five more mobs. So, I sent this arrow through the Nether repeatedly, letting me kill villagers infinitely without being nearby. And while this tech is cool, I couldn't find any real use for it aside from killing villagers. But this guy found one. Resin is both a useful temporary block and a nice building block, but it normally requires the player to obtain. This is because resin only spawns when a player damages a creaking. And there's no way to damage a creaking without the player being nearby until now. Because you can use the infinite arrow to hit the creaking to farm resin.

Mojang just made baby zombies easier to kill, added a new note block instrument, a bunch of new baby mob variants, and more in the newest Minecraft snapshot. Here's everything they added. First, a while ago, Mojang added sound variants to wolves, giving them even more customization than just their looks. And Mojang just added new sound variants to cats, pigs, cows, and chickens. Next, not just got a new instrument, the trumpet, which plays if you place a note block on a copper block. And the oxidization level of the copper also changes the sound of the trumpet. Next, a bunch more baby mobs got their textures and models changed, so they're no longer just smaller versions of their adults. Baby zombies, husks, and drowns look pretty much the same. Piglins and zombified piglins have cartman's build now, and baby villagers and zombie villagers got the bottom part of their face chopped off for some reason. And finally, their hit boxes all got much bigger.

A bunch more new baby animal textures and models and more just got added in the newest Minecraft snapshot. Here's everything that changed. First, Mojang made a bunch of changes to the way worlds are saved. And hopefully there's a good reason for this because it comes with a downside because the data is stored differently after you upgrade your world to this version. You won't be able to downgrade your world at all. This means you can't go back in time to get god armor anymore. And while someone might find a way around this, it does make it much harder. Next, Mojang removed the baby camel husk from the game, which is fine because you couldn't get them in survival anyway. But it is suspicious that they didn't remove baby zombie and skeleton horses despite also not being able to get them in survival. And they even got new textures and models recently, so I think we're bound to get a new way to get babies of them soon. And finally, there are new textures for baby bees, foxes, goats, camels, armadillos, polar bears, and llamas. Subscribe.

The spear just got a huge nerf and a huge buff in the newest Minecraft snapshot. Here's what changed. Spears do a lot of damage, allowing you to three-shot a player in full protection for Netherite when flying with fireworks. But you could kill players even faster because there's an enchantment for the mace called breach, which bypasses armor. And people quickly figured out that if you held a breach mace in your main hand and a spear in your off hand, it would do the damage of the spear while applying the mace's breach, doubling the amount of damage the spear did. You could also hold a windbururst mace, which let you pogo stick on enemies. Or if you stacked a bunch of armor stands, you could wind burst hundreds of thousands of blocks into the air. But Mojang just fixed this by making the enchantments in your main hand not affect the spear. But Mojang also gave the spear a huge buff because the lunge enchantment takes 128 durability per lunge and was incompatible with mending, meaning you'd have to make a new spear pretty often. But Mojang just changed this.

Mojang just buffed zombies in the newest Minecraft snapshot. For years, zombies have had 10 hearts, but there's been a secret zombie type that you've probably killed without even knowing, and it's supposed to have way more health. It's called a leader zombie, and it looks exactly the same as a normal zombie, but when you attack it, it has a really high chance to spawn in other zombies nearby that will help it kill you. Leader zombies can only spawn in hard difficulty, and they have up to a 5% chance of spawning depending on the local difficulty of the area. And they're supposed to be really tanky, but they weren't because of a bug. Basically, Mojang added a random multiplier to leader zombies maximum health, allowing them to have up to 50 hearts. But this was just the maximum health they could have, and Mojang forgot to actually set their health to this maximum. So even if they could have 50 hearts, they'd spawn with 10, just like other zombies. But Mojang just fixed this. So now you can run into 50 heart zombies. And if you're unlucky, they can have weapons, armor, and be babies.

There's a new cape in Minecraft to celebrate the copper age drop. And the elytra looks amazing. You have until October 20th at 1000 p.m. PDT to get it, and it should be possible to get multiple codes. Here's how to get it. There are three easy ways, but the fastest is currently to go on TikTok and watch a Minecraft stream for 3 minutes. This cape icon will show up in the top corner and then a timer shows up and just wait until it's done. Claim the code and go to minecraft.net/redeem and type in the code and your cape should show up on Bedrock. But be patient because it might take some time. The cape will probably also come to Java at some point because even though it doesn't explicitly say that, every other cape has come to Java eventually. The next way is to launch Bedrock Minecraft. It has to be Bedrock and place down a copper golem statue. It doesn't matter if you use creative mode to do this. And that's all you have to do. But this isn't necessarily the fastest way to do it because it says you'll get your cape sent to you by October 31st. So you might be waiting a while before you get the cape. And finally, you can watch a Minecraft stream on Twitch for 5 minutes. But this doesn't work yet, but I'll update you when it does.

animals staying babies forever and more just got added in the newest Minecraft snapshot. Here's everything that changed. First, Mojang has been changing the textures and models of a bunch of baby mobs. And now Mojang just did a bunch more. Baby axelottles, dolphins, turtles, squids, and glow squids all got updated textures and models. Also, the hitbox of baby squids, glow squids, and axelottals got bigger to better match their visuals. Baby chickens also got new sounds. But most importantly, Mojang added a new flower type, the golden dandelion. It doesn't currently spawn naturally, but it can be crafted using a dandelion and eight golden nuggets, and it can be placed like a normal flower or turned into yellow dye, but its main use is on baby animals. If you feed a golden dandelion to a baby mob, it will stop it from aging, but this only works on baby animals, not baby hostile mobs or villagers. And if you want it to start aging again, you can feed it another golden dandelion and will age like normal.

This is one of the coolest Minecraft websites I've ever seen. And it works on both Java and Bedrock. It's called Creative Mode, and it lets you create brand new mobs, items, and blocks without any coding. So, if you've ever wanted something added to Minecraft, you can add it easily. I literally changed majors from computer science because it was too hard. So, being able to mod the game with just text prompts is insane. Like, I made this sound staff that plays bell noises and knocks enemies away with its sound. And there's a whole library of other mods that people made. like this one just adds colored water to the game, which is so cool and should just be in Minecraft to begin with. And this one shouldn't be in Minecraft, but it's really fun anyway because it adds an Iron Man suit of armor to the game that lets you shoot rockets at people and even adds this awesome HUD screen when you wear the full set. It can add crafting recipes for the items and blocks and make custom mobs drop custom items. It's AI powered, so it's obviously not perfect, but it does a pretty great job and is so much fun to play around with. And if it isn't perfect, you can ask it to change the mod to your liking. Check out creativeote.net

The bundle has a secret feature that I've never seen anyone talk about, and it could be super useful in Dream Six Hunter Manhunt. Let me explain. Everyone knows bundles are insanely useful since they let you store a bunch of small stacks of items in one inventory slot instead of having them clutter your inventory. And while this obviously could be useful to Dream, it's not what I'm talking about. One of the best parts of Manhunt is when Dream outsmarts the hunters. But outsmarting the hunters can be really hard because Minecraft gives the hunters clues on what Dream is doing through advancements. Like if Dream gets iron, diamonds, obsidian, or blaze rods, the game prints a message in chat letting the hunters know that Dream has those items. But it's possible to avoid this because if you open a container and put items from it into a bundle and then put the bundle into your inventory, the advancement won't trigger. You can even craft using these items without triggering an advancement if you don't let the items into these slots. And this means Dream could gather a bunch of diamonds without the hunters knowing and then surprise them by suddenly having diamond gear.

Trident just became way rarer on Bedrock Edition. Let me explain. Trident are a pretty difficult item to obtain on Java because only about 6% of drowns spawn with the trident. And when you kill one with looting three, only 11.5% actually drop the trident. Meaning on average, you'll have to kill 144 drowns before you'll actually get a trident drown to drop its trident. But this wasn't that big of a deal because trident aren't that useful on Java. But on Bedrock, they are since tons of mob farms use trident killers to kill mobs with looting infinitely. And they're really strong since impaling does extra damage to anything that's wet. Luckily, since trident are so useful, bedrock drowns spawned with trident almost three times as often as on Java. And bedrock drowns dropped the trident over twice as frequently, making trident pretty easy to get. But even though it's worse for bedrock players, Mojang just made a parody change in the newest version. So drowns now spawn with trident as rarely as on Java.

Mojang might have been secretly cooking. So, Mojang has been releasing a bunch of snapshots lately, and let's be honest, most of them don't really add much. Sure, the new baby mobs are cute, but they don't really add new things to do in the game. So, a lot of people have been disappointed with this drop. But Mojang may have been cooking behind the scenes, which might be why this drop was so small, because they just revealed that the rendering engine used for Java is going to change in the next few months. And if you don't know what this means, it's basically how the code turns into visuals on the screen. And changing this will be both amazing and terrible. First, it means vibrant visuals for Java is still being worked on even though it's been a while since they announced it. Next, it means the people who speculate that Microsoft will discontinue Java are most likely wrong because they probably wouldn't spend so much time making Java future proof just to scrap it. And finally, it means Java may have some massive and much needed performance boosts. The one bad thing though is changing the rendering engine will break tons of mods and shaders.

Why does Mojang not bug test Bedrock? Bedrock has gotten several updates recently that have made the game completely unplayable for anyone on a computer. First, there's a bug that's causing insane amounts of lag, and it seems somehow tied to this random setting. But it gets even worse because now Bedrock has a memory leak, which basically means that over time it will start using all of your computer's memory until it lags horribly and crashes. But it still gets worse because Bedrock recently had an update that required PC players to uninstall Minecraft and reinstall it. But what some players didn't know was that this deleted everything related to Minecraft and Mojang didn't say anything about this. So loads of players lost their worlds. But if you can deal with all of those things, Mojang made it worse and Bedrock is completely unplayable. Now, this is because normally when you play Minecraft, your cursor is invisible but stays in the middle of the screen. But Bedrock now has a bug where your cursor moves when you move your head. This basically means that anytime you move around, your cursor can go off of your Minecraft window, and if you click, it pauses the game, causing you to

Mojang just said that the creeper wouldn't be added in today's Minecraft, and that's so stupid. One of Mojang's rules in Minecraft is that everything should be technically the player's fault, so they won't add natural disasters or things like that. This is a good way to design the game, but Mojang is taking it way too far, making future game drops less interesting. The main example is the creeper, which apparently wouldn't be added in today's Minecraft because getting blown up by a creeper is somehow not the player's fault. You can light up an area, sleep through the night, use a shield, or just pay attention to your surroundings to avoid getting blown up by creepers. If you don't do these things, then it's your fault that you got blown up. But somehow Mojang thinks this isn't the player's fault. The only reason it isn't the player's fault is if they don't know how a creeper works. But that's fine if they don't. You can learn how a creeper works by getting blown up. Failure is a part of the game. Not knowing how a mob works is such a broad definition of what isn't the player's fault that so many beloved features wouldn't get added now. And that's a tragedy because unexpected things happening in the game makes the game more interesting.

Mojang just made one of the weirdest changes to librarian villagers that nobody asked for. Let me explain. In the next Minecraft update, Mojang finally made name tags craftable. And this is pretty great since name tags are super useful. But it did have one negative. The highest level trade of librarian villagers became useless because rather than getting loads of resources to level librarians all the way up to unlock the trade, you could just craft a name tag with a piece of paper and a nugget. Fortunately, Mojang saw this and changed it. And since leveling a librarian villager all the way up takes so many resources, you'd think the master librarian would have something really good, like a guaranteed mending trade, which would also help with how infuriating for mending trades is, but no. For whatever reason, Mojang gave master librarians a guaranteed red and yellow candle trade. This is even worse than them just trading name tags.

Pandas have a secret breeding mechanic that I bet you didn't know. Let me explain. Everyone knows that pandas in Minecraft have a few different variants: [music] normal, lazy, aggressive, worried, playful, weak, and rarest of all, brown. But what you probably don't know is that panda breeding is way more complicated than every other mob in the game. Most mobs follow a simple rule. If the parents are different variants, the baby has a 50/50 chance of looking like either one. But with pandas, you can breed two normal pandas and get a brown baby. And this isn't a random mutation like how breeding two axelottals has a tiny chance to result in a blue axelottle. It's because pandas have two genes, one visible and one hidden. When pandas breed, each parent gives one random gene to its baby. Most genes are dominant, but weak and brown are recessive. Dominant genes mean if a baby gets both a dominant and recessive gene, the dominant gene is visible. But rarely the baby can get two recessive genes and that's when a weaker or brown panda is

What's the best villager for getting emeralds? You're probably thinking of the Fletcher, but in most cases, it's actually the mason. Let me prove it to you. Fletchers are pretty good for getting emeralds because you can sell them sticks, and sticks are pretty easy to get a lot of. But masons are even better because they buy clay, and clay is even easier to get than sticks. For example, if you wanted a stack of emeralds using a Fletcher, you'd need to chop down a ton of trees, which would take you about 5 minutes in a dense forest. But to get a stack of emeralds using a mason, you only need to mine a few clay deposits, which only takes about 4 minutes. And many villages spawn without loads of trees nearby. But rivers spawn everywhere, meaning there's almost always tons of clay nearby. And clay deposits are huge, getting you stacks of clay per deposit instead of a tiny bit of wood per tree. And if you still don't believe me, to get a stack of emeralds, you have to mine four stacks of logs or only two and a half stacks of clay blocks. And clay mines faster than wood.

Parrots are insanely overpowered and it makes no sense. I've talked before about why parrots are an awful pet because they do literally nothing except mimic mobs, which is really annoying, but I just saw this video from Vavilli that showed how parrots are actually useful when mining. Normally, you can only hear mobs from up to 16 blocks away. So, when digging around looking for a cave, you might not be able to hear mobs in caves if they're too far away. But parrots can hear mobs and start mimicking them from up to 20 blocks away, expanding the distance you can hear a cave slightly. And this is really smart. So I started researching parrots and found out they're way stronger than I thought. For instance, when you throw an ender pearl while riding a mob, the mob doesn't teleport with you. But parrots are the only mob in the game that actually do. But even stronger than that, parrots are completely invincible when riding a player's shoulder because they literally stop existing and don't have a hitbox when riding a player. But strongest of all, on Bedrock, parrots can control interdimensional travel because a parrot on your shoulder will stop you from going through Nether and End portals.

What's the best way to get enchanted golden apples? You're probably saying trial chambers because an ominous vault has the highest chance of giving you a notch apple in the game. But that doesn't actually mean that trial chambers are the best way. So to find out the best way, I raided both trial chambers and ancient cities as fast as I possibly could. Trial chambers took me about 20 minutes to complete since you have to kill tons of mobs to get vault keys. But ancient cities are way faster because you can just fly from chest to chest and loot the ancient city in about 4 minutes. And if you fly from chest to chest, you don't have to worry about wardens since you leave them behind outside of tracking distance. Additionally, I calculated the average amount of notch apples per structure and found that trial chambers give you an average of just above one notchapple per structure and ancient cities give almost three per structure. So ancient cities must be the winner, right? Well, not exactly because trial chambers are everywhere and ancient cities are super rare. So if you're using something like chunk base to locate structures, then ancient cities are way better. But if you're finding them completely legit, trial chambers might be faster because of the lack of travel

A new dimension was just discovered inside vanilla Minecraft, and it's really weird. Let me explain. We all know about the overworld Nether and end, but a YouTuber named Myron Urio just found a way to get to an insane new dimension in 1.12.2. They're calling it the ghost dimension. And to get there, you need to do some crazy bugs. First, you need to go to the end and trick the game into keeping thousands of rails powered, even though they shouldn't be. Then, you break the sides of the end portal frame and walk into the side of the portal. When you go through the portal, all these rails update at the same time, which overloads the game, causing you to get stuck on this loading screen. But if you hit escape and teleport away, you'll enter the ghost dimension, which is the end, but with a visual glitch, causing it to look like the bottom part of the overworld, but with these giant floating cubes. Some of them are normal chunks from the overworld, some are random blocks, and some are patterns. But weirder than that, none of these blocks are real. So if you rightclick a block, it disappears. And if you place down a block, a giant cube of that block appears.

There are new insanely rare mobs in Minecraft, and most people won't get one for an entire year. Let me explain. The newest Minecraft snapshot added the parched, which is a new desert variant of a skeleton, and the camel husk, which can have a husk and a parched riding. And once per year on Halloween, zombies, skeletons, and their variants have a small chance to spawn with pumpkins or jacko'lanterns on their heads. Pumpkins can be put on mob's head year round, but jacko'lanterns can't. So, getting a mob with one on its head is pretty rare with only a 2.5% chance of spawning. But with the camel husk, we can get an even rarer combination of mobs. When a camel husk spawns, it has a 5% chance to have a baby riding it and a 2.5% chance of the baby wearing a jacko'-lantern on its head and another 2.5% chance that the parched will also have a jacko'lantern [music] on its head. So, one in 32,000 camel husks will spawn like this. And this is only available for 1 day.

New horse armor, a zombie Nautilus variant, spear changes, and more just came in the newest snapshot. First, we just got confirmation from Mojang that these are the final features of the drop. So, rest in peace Hoglin and polar bear jockeyies. Next, there's a new advancement for when you spear five enemies with a single charge attack. You can use five of the same mobs, and you don't have to kill them, and you can currently cheese this advancement pretty hard by just using armor sticks. Next, the spear got a small nerf, making it harder to hit mobs with it. Previously, the charge attack was pretty forgiving, letting the player hit mobs from up to four pixels away from their hitboxes. But now, the player has to be within two pixels of the hitbox, making it slightly harder to land a charge attack. Next, the zombie nautilus just got a new variant that spawns in warm oceans. These are exactly the same as the regular zombie nautilus, but have coral growing out of them. And finally, we got Netherite horse armor, which can be made like you'd expect and is way better than diamond horse armor with eight more armor points. With diamond horse armor, a netherite sword crit would do just over nine damage.

What's the worst villager in Minecraft? You might think of the librarian that refused to give me the right trade for 30 minutes in this video, but there's actually a much less useful villager, the leather worker. First, leather workers trade you leather armor, which is obviously the worst armor in the game. But it gets worse because this armor is really overpriced. You're mainly paying for the fact that leather workers dye the armor so you can be fashionable. And this would be kind of cool if they made custom clothing that can only be acquired from them, like how the ominous banner is only obtainable by killing a raid captain. But they just choose two random dyes for the leather armor. And you could easily do that or use even more dyes for more customization. And even worse, these guys are pretty much useless for selling to because they only buy leather, rabbit hide, and turtle scoot. And who has tons of those? Maybe now that rabbits are little chunguses, you could get rabbit hide easier, but it's just not worth it. But worst of all, there used to be one reason to trade with leather workers. If you spend almost 100 emeralds to level a leather worker up, it will sell you a saddle, which isn't even useful anymore because they're craftable

This hilarious new prank just became possible in Minecraft because of the newest snapshot. Let me explain. To get food, loads of people will get two of an animal, fence them in, and then start breeding them. And animals take 20 minutes to grow up. So, most people will leave the area and do something more valuable with their time while they wait. And this gives you the chance to do a little trolling. Mojang just added the golden dandelion to Minecraft. And this stops baby animals from growing up if you feed them one. Eternal babies are visually exactly the same as normal babies. And the only way to tell if it's an eternal baby is by trying to feed it. Because eternal babies won't accept food. So, if you feed the babies golden dandelions without your friends seeing, they'll go insane, not knowing why their babies refuse to grow

Nobody uses allays anymore, but they should because they have a secret use that makes them more useful now than they ever were. Let me explain. Everyone knows allays pick up items for you, which can be somewhat helpful for sorting items. But since the copper golem came out and sorts items way better, people have abandoned their allays, but allays are still super useful for transporting mobs because of the chase the skies update. Transporting mobs is always a hassle, and chase the skies made it much easier, but also much slower. This is because you used to be able to fly with an elytra with mobs led to you, which was fast but hard because if you flew wrong, the lead would break. But in Chase the Skies, Mojang removed this by making the lead break instantly when you use a firework and replaced it with the Happy Ghast, which makes transporting mobs easy but extremely slow. But there's an easy way to transport mobs much faster because Mojang let you lead mobs to other mobs in this update. And if you give an allay an item, it will follow you around pretty quickly as long as you stay within 64 blocks of it. And if you lead mobs to the allay, you can fly with your elytra and have the allay bring the mobs around really quickly.

What's the goal of Hightail? Hightail came out in early access a few days ago, and I've had a ton of people ask me what the main goals are, and if there's a final boss, like the Ender Dragon in the game. So, here are the main things to do. When you first spawn in, there will be a forgotten temple gateway nearby. Go to it, kill the golem guarding the portal, and then go into it. Then go to this thing and interact with it. And this will unlock one of the major goals of the game, memories. Anytime you encounter a new creature, it will add it to your memories. So, it's kind of like Pokémon where you can encounter them all. And if you encounter a 100 creatures, you'll unlock some new dimensions. But those dimensions are dangerous. So, you'll probably need some good gear. So, another goal is to get the best gear in the game, which is currently at a mantite and requires several hours of work and trips to dangerous biomes to get. And then once you have 100 memories and some good gear, you can craft keys to three new dungeons. These dungeons are like the major biomes, but a whole world of it. And they have a time limit on them. They're work in progress, though.
